segments.text:
The Immune System
CLS-N390 Vaccines: S c ience and Society

L e arning Objectives
Describe how the immune system responds to pathogens.
03
Identify and describe the functions of the cells and organs of the immune system.
01
Compare and contrast the types of immunity that are seen.
02
What is the purpose of the immune system?
Self from non-self
Major Lines of Defense
O r gans of the Immune System

Organs and Tissue
Skin
Largest immune organ of the body
Skin cells (keratinocyte) secrete peptides known as defensins
Sebaceous and sweat glands produce lactic acid and fatty acids
Immune cells live here
Barrier immunity = first line of defense
Stop the germs before the can get in
Mucosal Surfaces
Respiratory tract – cilia (hairs) sweep thing out; mucous produced
Genital tract – lactic acid produced; mucous produced
Gastrointestinal tract – mucous produced; host bacteria defend their turf
Immune cells live in all places

Organs and Tissue
Bone Marrow
Where immune cells are created
Stem cells  specific cell types
Thymus
Located in the upper chest
Where T cells mature and become activated
Spleen
Largest internal organ of immune system
25% of blood from heart go through spleen on every beat
Cells are filtered for pathogens

Organs and Tissue
Bloodstream
Immune cells constantly circulate, patrolling for problems
Transfers immune cells to organs to fight
White blood cells are measured in blood to indicate problems
Lymphatic System
Network of vessels and tissue – lymph, extracellular fluid, lymph nodes
Communication hubs where pathogens are brought by circulating cells to activate more cells
Cells of the Immune System
Types of Immunity
Innate Immunity

Innate Immunity
An antigen is any substance, living (bacteria, virus, etc.) or inactivated (protein), that elicits a response from the immune system.
Innate immunity = natural immunity
Does not require prior exposure to an antigen
Can respond immediately to an invader
Recognizes broadly distributed antigen molecules rather than specific ones
Key players = white blood cells (leukocytes)
Come from myeloid progenitor cell
Collectively referred to as phagocytes or phagocytic cells = eat things
Granulocytes
Have granules containing substances to destroy invaders
Plymophonuclear lymphocytes
Because the nucleus has multiple lobes

Neutrophils
40% to 70% of total circulating white blood cells
F irst line of defense against infection – bacteria
Mature neutrophils have a half-life of about 2 to 3 days
During acute inflammatory responses (such as infection)  leave the peripheral blood circulation and enter tissues where the problem is occurring
Purpose = engulf and digest pathogens (phagocyte)

Eosinophils
U p to 5% of circulating white blood cells
Target organisms too large to be engulfed
K ill by secreting toxic substances ( eg , reactive oxygen compounds similar to those produced in neutrophils), major basic protein (which is toxic to parasites), eosinophil cationic protein, and several enzymes
Major source of inflammatory mediators = allergies

Basophils
< 5% of circulating white blood cells
Basophils have high-affinity receptors for IgE (a type of antibody) = triggers release of granules when bound by antigen
Release histimine = "allergy feeling" we have with seasonal allergies

Monocytes
Patrol and respond to problems
Macrophages, "big eater" in Greek, ingest and degrade bacteria
Monocytes and macrophages coordinate an immune response by notifying other immune cells of the problem.
Housekeepers = recycle dead cells, like red blood cells, and clearing away cellular debris
Monocyte in blood
Macrophage in tissue

Dendritic cells
Phagocytic leukocyte that circulates in the blood or takes up residence in the peripheral tissues
E ssential for immune surveillance and activation of adaptive immune cells
A ntigen presenting cells = engulf antigens and display them as small fragments on their surface to activate T lymphocytes

Natural Killer Cells
Subset of lymphocytes
I mportant for tumor surveillance
E xpress both activating and inhibitory receptors
Healthy cells – have markers that inhibit NK cells from killing
Virus infected cells and cancer cells – do not have markers = NK cells at tack!
Adaptive Immunity

Acquired Immunity
Natural acquired immunity
Naturally encounter a foreign invader  develop antibodies
Artificial acquired immunity
Intentionally exposed to foreign elements  develop antibodies

Lymphocytes
18% to 42% of circulating cells
Lymphocytes are divided into three major groups
T cells
B cells
N atural killer (NK) cells – part of innate immunity; you saw them.
major players in adaptive immunity

T lymphocytes
Recognize and remove virus infected cells and cancer cells
Have granules containing cytotoxins that cause apoptosis = cell death
Cytotoxic T cells (CD8)
Produce cytokines (signal proteins)
Activate other immune cells against bacteria and parasites
Alert B cells to make antibodies
Prevent adverse immune actions
Helper T cells (CD4)

B lymphocytes
Develop in bone marrow
Circulate as naïve cell (no surface receptors)
Eat antigens and create receptors
Future encounter = I know you!  activation
Make antibodies
Antibodies

Proteins = immunoglobulins
Created in response to a pathogen seen by the immune system
Antibodies

Immunoglobulin G
Gamma ( γ ) heavy chain
Monomer shape = one antibody
Short name = IgG
Binding sites = 2
Most abundant – 80% of circulating antibodies
Crosses placenta
Binds to phagocytes
Main antibody of secondary response
Vaccines target the production of these antibodies

Immunoglobulin M
Mu ( µ ) heavy chain
Pentamer shaper = five antibodies attached at Fc regions in a ring
Short name = IgM
Binding sites = 10
6% of circulating antibodies
Does not cross placenta
Main antibody of primary response
Monomer of IgM = B cell receptor

Immunoglobulin A
Alpha ( α ) heavy chain
Dimer shaper = two antibodies connected at Fc region
Short name = IgA
Binding sites = 4
13% of circulating antibodies
Does not cross placenta
Secreted in mucous, tears, saliva, colostrum

Immunoglobulin E
Epsilon ( ε ) heavy chain
Monomer shaper = one antibody
Short name = IgE
Binding sites = 2
0.002% of circulating antibodies
Does not cross placenta
Binds to mast cells and basophils
Allergy and antiparasitic activity

Immunoglobulin D
Delta ( δ ) heavy chain
Monomer shaper = one antibody
Short name = IgD
Binding sites = 2
1% of circulating antibodies
Does not cross placenta
B cell receptor

How to antibodies actually work?
The very tip of the antibody (variable region) is unique to the antigen the antibody was made against
Occasionally, we seen incidences where antibodies are made against a pathogen and the antigen binding site is so close to surface markers on our own healthy organs that the antibodies unintentionally attack. One classic example of this is when someone has strep throat that is not treated, the body creates antibodies to fight the infection. Unfortunately, the antigen binding site of these antibodies also binds to markers on the surface of the heart. This causes rheumatic fever.

What do antibodies do?
Neutralization: Antibodies neutralize pathogens by binding to them and blocking or inhibiting their binding or interaction with cells that they may infect.
For example, when HIV infects a cell it binds to surface markers on the cell that allow it to enter and cause infection. Antibodies against HIV bind to the virus, preventing it from attaching to the surface markers on cells.

What do antibodies do?
Opsonization: This action occurs when antibodies coat the pathogen, tagging it for phagocytosis by innate immune cells.
Many innate immune cells (monocytes, macrophages, neutrophils) have Fc receptors that recognize the constant region of antibodies, which allows them to engulf the pathogen and destroy it.

What do antibodies do?
Complement activation: Antibodies can activate complement through the classic pathway which recruits complement proteins to the pathogen for destruction.
Complement is a complex pathway to understand and well beyond the scope of his course but essentially when all of the proteins come together they create a hole in the pathogen causing it to burst (lyse) and fall apart.
The Immune Response

First encounter
Lag phase – may take a few days to a week
When cells begin to know there is something foreign around
Antigen presenting cells show antigen to T cells  activate B cells
Log phase – lots of B cells made
Antibodies begin made – IgM first, then IgG
Some B cells become memory cells
Second encounter
Anamnestic response – B cells remember!
IgM is made quickly
Lots of IgG made faster than first time
Battle won much quicker than first time
Vaccines act as first encounter

The infection
Let’s see the immune system in action

How does it start?
It’s a beautiful day when suddenly a rusty nail appears and cuts you
The first barrier is broken, nearby bacteria seize on the opportunity an enter
Rusty nail
Your skin

Growing the infection
Go, go, go!

Thanks to macrophages, the immune system will be able to stop this invasion as fast as they can.
You’ll see!
The defense to the attack

Next you will notice an inflammation and heat. I ncreasing the temperature tries to kill th e bacteria and increasing bloodflow brings more cells to fight.
The macrophages’ defense

To kill the bacteria you will need reinforcements! For this reason, the macrophages bind to the neutrophils that after fighting against the bacteria will die
Byeeee
The Neutrophils

It is time for reinforcements, the dendritic cell is provided with antibacterials to end the infection and act as antigen presenting cells.
The dendritic cell

To kill the invaders, the dendritic cell must join a T cell, in this way they can kill the enemy and end this infection
Virgin helper T cell
Binding parts
Dendritic cell

B cell
T helper cell
Yeah!
Stimulating cells
Keep going!

B cells make antibodies to join in the battle.
B Cell
The antibodies
Antibody

Not now!
I’m tired...
The helper T cells

Then the second line of defense arrives, millions of antibodies render or kill the intruders in the process
They also stun the bacteria and make them an easy target connecting to killer cells
Another line of defense

Balance shifts
The time to balance the damage has come, the infection has been defeated and many cells have lost their lives in the way, but now you are immune and stronger!
Bye friends!

Memory B cell
Memory cells
These cells will be prepared to fight. In case the enemy appears again they will attack it even before you notice.
Memory helper T cell

How Diseases Spread
CLS-N390 Vaccines: S c ience and Society

Describe how humans and microbes interact with each other.
01
Compare and contrast the types of symbiosis seen between hosts and microbes.
02
Describe the human microbiota.
03
Compare and contrast modes of disease transmission.
04
L e arning Objectives
What is an infectious disease?

Bidirectional interaction
Humans use microbes
Food and fermentation
Biological insecticides
Genetic engineering
Biodegrading
Microbes use humans
Food
Shelter
dissemination

Fetus = sterile
Microorganisms (microbes) introduced during delivery and first few days after birth
Colonization – growth of microbes in or on the body without damage or symptoms
Normal flora = found on or in body of healthy people
Resident = colonize area for months to years
Transient = visitors that do not stay
Carrier state = ability to transmit organisms
Origin of Microbiota

Host and microbe benefit from relationship
Microbe benefits but no harm or benefit to host
Microbe benefits and host is harmed
Symbiosis

Bacteria
Viruses
Fungi
Parasites
Microorganisms
How do microbes spread?

Direct contact with the tissue or fluids of an infected individual
Mucous membranes
Open wounds
Abraded skin
Bites or scratches
Most common and highest risk route of pathogen transmission
Direct contact transmission

Inanimate objects contaminated by an infected person come in contact with susceptible person
Exam tables
Medical equipment
Environmental surfaces
Clothing
Fomite transmission

Also referred to as airborne transmission – tiny particles or droplet nuclei
Released by breathing, coughing, sneezing, or vocalizing
Released during medical procedures like suctioning, dentistry, or inhalation anesthesia
May be inhaled, deposited onto surfaces, or onto mucous membranes
Aerosol transmission

Occurs when contaminated food or water is ingested
Environmental contaminants most common – exudates, feces, urine, saliva
Contaminated objects may transmit when licked or chewed
Oral (ingestion) transmission

Vectors = living organisms – mosquitoes, fleas, ticks, rodents, etc.
Transmission via bites most common
Can transfer pathogens to other animals or locations
Vector-borne transmission

The science of vaccines
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and S o ciety

Learning Objectives
Incorporate all components of a vaccine to build one.
Describe what a vaccine is.
Compare and contrast the type of vaccines produced and under investigation.
Diagram the process used to evaulate vaccines for safety and efficacy.
01
03
02
04
Apply the scientific method to a given scenario.
05

What is a vaccine?
A biological product that can be used to safely induce an immune response that confers protection against infection and/or disease on subsequent exposure to a pathogen (Pollard & Bijker , 2021, p. 83).
Does not cause disease
Artificially mimics infection
Generates primary immune response = creation of memory B cells
How does a vaccine work?

How have vaccines helped?
Pollard, A. J., & Bijker , E. M. (2020). A guide to vaccinology: from basic principles to new developments. Nature Reviews Immunology , 1-18.
Types of Vaccines
Vaccine Categories

Live vaccines
No modification of virus or bacteria
Given small amount of infectious agent – mild disease occurs
No longer used
Example: smallpox
Modified to a weakened or inactivated state – remove part that is pathogenic
Widely used – elicits immune response
Examples: measles, mumps, rubella, chicken pox, influenza, oral polio….
Live active
Live attenuated

Non-living vaccines
Whole cell used – killed to render nonviable
Examples: whole-cell pertussis, influenza, polio, hepatitis A, rabies, 1896 - typhoid
Modification of toxin produced by bacteria
Anti-toxoid antibody production
Short lived ~10 years
Examples: tetanus, 1923 - diphtheria
Whole killed
Toxoid

Non-living vaccines
Purified protein, recombinant protein, polysaccharide, peptide
Only uses a portion of the pathogen to create an immune response
Examples: pertussis, influenza, hepatitis B, meningococcus, pneumococcus, hepatitis A, 1970 - anthrax
Created to look like the virus, but not be the virus
Examples: human papillomavirus, 1986 – hepatitis B
Subunit
Virus-like particle

Non-living vaccines
Gram negative bacteria outer cell  membrane with antigen attached
Used for Gram negative bacteria
Example: 1987 – Haemophilus influenzae type B
Polysaccharide chain used to elicit immune response – bound to carrier protein to make it travel better
Typically seen with bacteria
Example: Haemophilus influenzae type B, pneumococcal, typhoid,  1987 - meningococcal
Outer membrane vesicle
Protein-polysaccharide conjugate

Novel Vaccines
mRNA vaccines
Viral vector vaccines
Microneedle vaccines
The Scientific Method

The Scientific Method
Logical, problem-solving approach used by scientists
Typically starts with an observation that leads to a question and wanting to know more
Cancer biologist – might see that a certain kind of cancer cannot be treated with chemotherapy
Marine ecologist – might notice that a coral reef in the area is bleaching (turning white)
Steps to the Scientific Method
Make an observation
Form a question or identify a problem
Research – see if there are already answers out there
Form a hypothesis
Conduct experiment to test hypothesis
Draw conclusion from experiment data
Report interpretation
Why?

Scenario:
You are getting ready for an all-day study session about vaccines, and you need a good breakfast to get you started.
You are going to make a breakfast sandwich and coffee.
You put bread in the toaster, but it won’t toast.
The Scientific Method

You are a little frustrated because your eggs are now getting cold.
You grab your cup of coffee and ask…
The Scientific Method
Why?

You try to get the toaster to work and notice the light doesn’t come on and the coils do not get hot.
You make sure it is plugged in, and it is.
This makes you think…
The Scientific Method

To determine if your prediction that the outlet is broken is true, you need to come up with a hypothesis to test it.
A hypothesis isn’t always the right answer, but it is a way to help you determine the right answer.
The Scientific Method

You have made a hypothesis and now it is time to test it out.
Let's find another outlet and plug the toaster in.
The Scientific Method

Results of your test may support or contradict your hypothesis.
Support of hypothesis cannot conclusively provide it is correct but means that it is likely correct.
If results contradict a hypothesis, it is probably not true – unless there was a flaw in the test.
The Scientific Method
Vaccine Development

First step – observation of the need
Collaborative effort – academia, small biotech companies, large vaccine manufacturers, regulatory authorities/ government
Several obstacles to think about when creating a vaccine
Vaccine Development

Antigen
Blueprint that generates an immune response – must have!
Small portion of disease-causing organism – protein or polysaccharide
Toxin
DNA/RNA
Whole organism – weakened or attenuated

Preservatives
Prevent the vaccine from becoming contaminated once opened
Used for vaccines that have multiple doses – single dose vaccines do not have preservatives
Most common = 2-phenoxyethanol
Used for many years – safe and used in baby care products

Stabilizers
Prevent chemical reactions from occurring and keep vaccine components from sticking to vial
Regularly consumed for naturally produced by humans = no safety concern
Sugars (lactose, sucrose)
Amino acids (glycine)
Gelatin
Proteins (recombinant human albumin derived from yeast)

Surfactants
Reduce the surface tension of vaccine components
Acts as emulsifier = keeps ingredients blended together
Prevent settling and clumping
Used in many applications, such as ice cream

Diluent
Liquid used to dilute vaccine to correct concentration
Most common = sterile water

Residuals
Tiny amounts of various substance used during manufacturing – not active ingredients
Vary depending on manufacturing process – egg proteins, yeast, antibiotics
Measured in parts per million or parts per billion
Occasionally cause allergic reactions

Adjuvant
Improves immune response
Act by:
Keeping vaccine at injection site longer
Stimulating local immune cells
Providing way for vaccine to be absorbed
Most common = tiny amount of aluminum salts
Does not cause long-term health problems, regularly ingested through eating and drinking
Evaluating Vaccines for Efficacy and Safety

Pre-clinical Phase
Every vaccine begins at this phase. Vaccines are tested in an organ model to answer the following.
Is the vaccine safe? Adverse immune reactions are checked for in the established model organism.
What is a safe starting dose for humans? Multiple doses are tested to estimate the safest starting dose to use in humans during future phases.
Is this vaccine effective? This phase needs to show some efficacy in the animal model before it can ethically be tested in humans.
Typically, this phase takes approximately 1.5 years to complete and can cost an average of $20 million.
Success rate: 14% of vaccines make it thorough this phase.

Phase I
Phase I begins the initial trials in human subjects. Volunteers agree to be given the vaccine to test for safety and efficacy. Since this is the very first time the vaccine is given to people, we are talking about small test groups, only in the tens. In this phase, the following questions are answered.
Is the vaccine safe? Volunteers are checked for adverse immune system reactions, side effects, and evidence of toxicity.
What is the immune response in humans? The type of immune response and the extent of the immune response are determined in humans.
Typically, this phase takes several months to complete and can cost an average of $9 million.
Success rate: Approximately 32% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, which is only 4.5% of all vaccines that begin the clinical trial process.

Phase II
In this phase, volunteers are recruited from different age, gender, and ethnic backgrounds with various health statuses. Participants are placed in larger groups, typically in the hundreds. T he following questions are answered.
Is the vaccine still safe? The vaccine is continued to be assessed for safety and potential side effects are looked for in a more diverse study group.
How does the immune response differ among different people? The immune response is monitored, especially in regard to the subjects’
age, gender, and ethnicity. Additionally, the optimal dose is determined based on participant response to various doses.
What is the best way to administer the vaccine? The vaccine preparation and route of delivery are determined. Additionally, a schedule of immunization is created, which includes the age of first administration, number of doses, sequence of or interval between doses, duration of immunity, and potential need for booster immunizations.
This phase can take up to two years to complete and can cost anywhere from $9 million to $15 million.
Success rate: 50% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, which is only 2.2% of vaccines that begin the clinical trial process.

Phase III
Phase III is considered a long-term trial to catch any unexpected or rare events and to evaluate efficacy longer than the shorter, smaller phases. This phase recruits many more volunteers, in the thousands. The goal of this phase is to evaluate the vaccine in the most diverse population available, that should represent the population who will receive the vaccine. In this phase, the following questions are answered.
Is this vaccine still safe? The safety of the vaccine continues to be assessed and potential side effects or rare events are identified. This is performed for a longer duration in a more homogeneous target population.
This phase often lasts one to four years can cost anywhere from $40 million to $100+ million.
Success rate: At this point, 100% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, resulting in only 2.2% of vaccines that begin the clinical trials process seeking regulatory approval.
Is this vaccine effective? The clinical efficacy of the vaccine is determined, usually by monitoring the relative disease incidence among the vaccinated versus unvaccinated portion of the target population.

Regulatory Approval
After making it through phases I to III, the vaccine manufacturer must go before the regulatory authorities (often the FDA) for final approval before taking it to the market. During this review, the regulatory authorities review all data collected during all phases, specifically seeking evidence of safety and efficacy.
One in ten vaccines are not approved by the regulatory authorities and must continue data collection, or possibly scrap the entire vaccine study.
This phase takes an average of 1.32 years to complete and costs approximately $1.5 million to $2 million.
Success rate: 92% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, which is a only 2% of all vaccines that start the clinical trial process.

Phase IV
After a vaccine receives approval from regulatory authorities and is widely used in the general public, it enters what is referred to as phase IV. This is not a formal clinical trial phase that we are accustomed to, rather a continuous long-term assessment of the vaccine's efficacy and safety throughout the entire population. This continued assessment confirms that the clinical efficacy and safety determined during the clinical trials transfers to the general public.
At any time during this observation, if there are any concerns that are raised, the regulatory authorities may pause or completely stop the use of the vaccine until further investigation is completed to determine the impact of the concern.
COVID-19 Vaccine Timeline

CLS-N390 Vaccines: S c ience a nd Society
The History of Vaccination and Current Recommendations

L e arning Objectives
Recognize and describe the contributions of individuals in the advancement of vaccination practices.
01
02
Create a timeline of major events and efforts leading to current vaccination practices.
03
Review the history of modern vaccination recommendations.
04
Reflect on the receipt of vaccines as a child and adult for you and your family.
05
Compare and contrast current vaccine recommendations for children under 6 years old, 7-18 years old, and adults.

Introduction
The history of vaccination goes all the way back to 1000 AD when soars from smallpox survivors were scratched into the skin of others to provide immunity.

Terminology
Inoculation = introduction of material into a person to provoke immunity to a disease
Vaccination = introduction of a material designed to provoke an immune response that will provide protection from a related disease agent
Smallpox = disease caused by the variola virus
Variolation = smallpox immunization technique once practiced in many locations worldwide – transfer of matter from a smallpox sore into a cut in the skin of an uninfected person

Infectious Diseases and Vaccination up to Edward Jenner
Unknown fever struck English royal family, killing Prince Henry at 18
Didn’t know what it was – now know it was Salmonella typhi = typhoid fever
1612
French Jesuits knowingly bring smallpox to North America
Entire villages of Native Americans exterminated to due disease
1625
Smallpox outbreak hits colonial settlers in MA – 20 settlers from Mayflower
Devastated Native American population
John Winthrop, governor, said it was “divine intervention”
1633
Epidemic of yellow fever in Cuba and Yucatan  quarantine was accepted in America
Boston quarantines all ships from West Indies
Outbreak of measles in 1657
1648

Infectious Diseases and Vaccination up to Edward Jenner
Death of Chinese emperor from smallpox – new emperor supported inoculation
Inoculated his children and recommended continuation for all descendants
1661
Queen Mary II died of variola hemorrhagica = internal and pustule bleeding due to smallpox
Age 32
1659
Smallpox epidemic in Boston
Zabdiel Boylston inoculated 248 people, 6 died = 3% fatality rate
Smallpox killed 844 = 14% fatality rate
First variolation in America
1721
Benjamin Franklin lost son to smallpox in 1736
Large outbreak in Charleston, SC in 1738 killed 14% infected and half of Cherokee Indian population
1736 - 1738

Explosion of science and technology beginning mid-1800s lead to a lot of discoveries and advancement in infectious diseases and vaccination
You will continue the story

Vaccination Recommendations
Resources from CDC

Introduction to Vaccine Recommendations
Before 1985 – only seven vaccines recommended
Today – children under 2 years get vaccinated for 14 potentially serious diseases
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) makes vaccination schedule based on recommendations from Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) – approved by American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) and American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP)
Who makes recommendations?

Provided to ACIP for Recommendation
The safety and efficacy of the vaccine when given at specific ages.
Only vaccines licensed by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) are recommended, and vaccine makers must conduct rigorous tests during clinical trials to show that a vaccine is safe and effective at specific ages.
The severity of the disease.
Vaccines recommended for children are meant to prevent diseases that can be serious for them, potentially causing long-term health problems, or even death.
How many children get the disease if there is no vaccine.
Vaccines that do not provide benefit to many children may not be recommended.
The differences in how well a vaccine works for children of different ages.
The ability of vaccines to help the body produce immunity can vary depending on the age when the vaccine is given.
